coredata.dc:title: DNA biosensor based on surface modification of ITO by physical vapor deposition of gold and carbon quantum dots modified with neutral red as an electrochemical redox probe
coredata.dc:creator: Azizi, Sajad
Gholivand, Mohammad-Bagher
Amiri, Masoud
Manouchehri, Iraj
coredata.dc:description: Here, we report on an effective modified DNA biosensor. First, the surface of an indium tin oxide (ITO) is modified with gold via physical vapor deposition technique. Second, we also report on the synthesis of carbon dots, which are modified with neutral red as an electrochemical redox probe. The different steps of modifications are monitored with atomic force microscope, UV–Vis spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy FT-IR spectroscopy, dynamic light scattering and as well as different electrochemical techniques. The biosensor is applied for determination of salmonella DNA sequence at ultra-low concentrations with linear range of 20–240 fM and 10 fM limit of detection. The ability of detection of the target of biosensor is evaluated in spiked samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: DNA biosensor
Carbon dot
Neutral red
Physical vapor deposition
Salmonella

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of carbon dot nanoparticle modified with neutral red
Characteristics of the synthesized CDs and CDs modified with neutral red (CDs-Nr)
Characterization of the modified ITO electrodes
Simulated proof-of-concept demonstration using DNA fragments

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–Vis spectra (A), fluorescence (B) and FT-IR of Nr, CDs and CDs-Nr (C). The DLS digram of CDs and CDs-Nr (D).
The AFM images of (A) ITO, (B) Au/ITO (before heating treatment), (C) Au/ITO (after heating treatment) and (D) ssDNA/MER/Au/ITO.

(A) DPV responses of the ssDNA/MER/ITO electrode recorded in 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.4) and 0.1 mg mL−1 CDs-Nr, after incubation in the different concentrations of tDNA (0, 20, 60, 90, 120, 150, 180, 210 and 240 fM).
The schematic illustration for fabrication of (A) (CDs-Nr) and (B) DNA biosensor.
the EIS paramertersof the modified ITO electrodes. *

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: * kapp = RT/n2F2RctAC, Q = Cdl g[Rs-1 + Rct −1]1−g, where A: surface area of electrode, 0.0314 cm2, C: concentration of redox probe, 0.005 mM, R: gas constant, 8.315 J mol−1 K−1, T: Kelvin temprature, g: surface inhomogenous paramerter and for a complet smooth surface equls 1.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: DPV signal: Comparison of hybridization signal intensities for ssDNA/MER/AuNPs/ITO with 300 fM, complementary salmonella (CM), double-base mismatched (DMM) and non-complementary sequences (NCMM), respectively.
Compare results obtained by spiking in milk.
Compare the results of this work with other methods used to detect Salmonella DNA.

head.ce:title: DNA biosensor based on surface modification of ITO by physical vapor deposition of gold and carbon quantum dots modified with neutral red as an electrochemical redox probe

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry, Razi University, Kermanshah 671496734, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Chemistry, Razi University, Kermanshah 671496734, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Science, Department of Physics, Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Science, Department of Physics, Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, we report on an effective modified DNA biosensor. First, the surface of an indium tin oxide (ITO) is modified with gold via physical vapor deposition technique. Second, we also report on the synthesis of carbon dots, which are modified with neutral red as an electrochemical redox probe. The different steps of modifications are monitored with atomic force microscope, UV–Vis spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy FT-IR spectroscopy, dynamic light scattering and as well as different electrochemical techniques. The biosensor is applied for determination of salmonella DNA sequence at ultra-low concentrations with linear range of 20–240 fM and 10 fM limit of detection. The ability of detection of the target of biosensor is evaluated in spiked samples.

ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical biosensors have been presented as fast, inexpensive with high sensitivity and accuracy devices for determination of different analytes and biomarkers [1–3]. In a typical electrochemical biosensor, (such as aptasensors, genosensors or immunosensors), the analytical performance is directly controlled by three factors. First factor is the modified surface of a conductive electrode on which a recognition element is immobilized. Naturally, an ideal electrode should provide a versatile surface with high conductivity and ability for immobilization of a recognition element such as nucleic acids. Most of the time, (nano)materials such as graphene, conductive polymers and noble metals like Au, have used as such electrode materials [4–7]. Among various nano-materials, the gold nanostructures have been presented as important electrode materials due to their high conductivity, easy functionalization, catalytic traits and special biocompatibility [8]. Interestingly, the electrochemical properties of these gold nano-structures directly rely on their physical properties such as size and morphology [8,9]. In this regard, one method for fabricating of inexpensive and highly reproducible nanostructure of gold particles is the physical vapor deposition (PVD) [10]. On the other hand, the PVD technique provides an effective way for covering and producing scalable surface area with high electrochemical properties for practical applications.

The second factor is related to the immobilized recognition elements. Obviously, in order to detect different analytes like a target DNA or a biomarker, variety of recognition elements are available. However, also in this case, choosing a suitable surface with high surface area for immobilization of these recognition elements can significantly increase the sensitivity of a biosensor. Finally, even after a layer of recognition element successfully immobilized on a suitable surface, the analytical signal must be recorded. In fact, this analytical signal provides a logical relation between the changes of an electrochemical activity (e.g. current or resistance) and the concentration of an analyte via interaction of the analyte and the recognition elements. In this case, there are two approaches for recording an analytical signal. Frist is labeled techniques and the latter is the label-free techniques. The labeled techniques work based on the recording an analytical signal produced by an electroactive component such as molecule or enzyme that chemically attached to a probe groups (antibody or probe DNA etc.) [3,11]. Here, the most difficult step of manufacturing a labeled biosensor is engineering of a labeled bio-recognition element (e.g. producing an antibody labeled with enzyme or a DNA labeled with methylene blue). In the latter technique, the attachment of the analyte to its recognition element is brought about a detectable change in the analytical signal. In this case, the analytical signal can be recorded via changes in behavior of axillary probe (a probe mostly exist in the solution in which the electrode is located) like Fe(CN)6 4/3+. For example, the resistance of electrode (as the analytical signal) can change due to the attachment of a target DNA [12]. So far, many label free electrode by means of different nanoparticles (electroactive probes) have been presented. For some instances, by applying dendrimers-encapsulated silver nanocomposites that linked with DNA-encapsulated Ag nanoclusters [13], MXene (Ti3C2) nanosheets [14] thionine functionalized reduced graphene oxide [15] etc., some sensitive electrochemical biosensor were reported.

Most of the time, the preparation of these nanostructures (for producing analytical signal) have tiresome procedures with different steps and expensive reagents. Here, we report on a simple electrochemical label free electrochemical DNA biosensor based on modification of quantum carbon dots, CDs, with neutral red (Nr) as an electrochemical probe. Moreover, the surface of ITO electrode was modified by gold with PVD-deposition technique. This electrode has been used for electrochemical detection of Salmonella gene sequence as dangerous and famous food poisoning bacteria [16]. The CDs is not an electroactive specie, hence, we modified them via attachment of Nr as a highly electroactive organic molecule. Having this modification done, we presented an electrochemical probe, which not only showed high electrochemical activity but also, unlike the Nr that is highly adsorbed by electrode surface, showed insignificant fouling effect. The resulted biosensor has been successfully used for ultrasensitive detection of Salmonella sequence.

Nr (3-Amino-7-dimethylamino-2-methylphenazine hydrochloride), 2-mercaptoethanol and 6-mercaptohexanoic acid, 2-(N-morpholino) ethanesulfonic acid (MES), N-(3-Di-methylaminopropyl)-N′ethylcarbodiimidehydrochloride and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 98%) were obtained from sigma Aldrich (St.Louis,MO,USA). K3[Fe(CN)6] and K4[Fe(CN)6] were purchased from Merck (Darmstadt Germany). DNA oligonucleotides were from Fazabiotech Co.(Tehran, Iran):
Probe DNA (ssDNA): NH2-5′-GGAGCTGCTGGCATTATTGAA-3′.
Target DNA (tDNA): 5′-TTCAATAATGCCAGCAGCTCC-3′.
Double-base mismatched DNA: 5′-TTCAATAATGCTAGAAGCTCC-3′.
Non-complementary DNA: 5′-CATCTCATGGCCGATTCGTTG-3′.
All other materials were of analytical grade.

Electrochemical studies were performed by an Autolab system potentiostat galvanostat model PGSTAT 302N (Utrecht, Netherlands) equipped with a NOVA (1.11) software using ITO modified electrode as working electrode at room temperature. A platinum wire and saturated Ag/AgCl were utilized as an auxiliary and reference electrodes. The surface morphology properties were examined by the atomic force microscope (AFM) Nanosurf model mobile S. In order to monitor the hydrodynamic size distribution of nanoparticles dynamic light scattering (DLS) apparatus (Malvern, UK) was applied. A Cary Eclipse spectrofluorometer equipped with a quartz microcell (Agilent, USA) is used for fluorescence measurements with an excitation and emission slits were fixed at 5 nm. An Agilent 8453 diode array spectrophotometer (Agilent, USA) was used for UV–Vis studies over the range of 200 nm to 800 nm.

CDs was synthesized via a solvothermal method [17] by dissolving 4.0 g of glucose in 100 mL of double-distilled water and transferring the solution in an autoclave. Then, the autoclave was heated at 180 °C (for 10 h) and after the cooling at room temperature, the resulted solution showed an orange brown color indicating the formation of the CDs. The mixture (from autoclave process) was centrifuged at 10000 rpm and the supernatant was filtered through a 0.45 μm filter.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon dot nanoparticle modified with neutral red
ce:section.ce:para: 20 mL of PBS solution (1 mM, pH 6) containing 15 mM NHS and 30 mM EDC.HCl was added to 50 mL of CDs solution and stirred for 20 min in order to activate the carboxyl groups of CDs. Then, 5.0 mg of neutral red was added to the activated solution and stirred for 40 min. The resulting particles (CDs-Nr) were centrifugally at 10,000 rpm and the precipitated particles were separated, washed and dispersed in double distilled water. This process was repeated three times to remove the free Nr. Finally, the CD-Nr were dried and stored for later use.

Au nanoparticles were deposited on flexible ITO substrate by thermal evaporation in vacuum. To attain high purity Au thin films, before deposition process, the chamber pumped down to 9 × 10−6 mbar and then tiny Au granules (99.99% in purity) were melted in a tungsten boat to evaporate and deposit on the ITO substrates. The deposition performed in 30 s then the as deposited electrode transferred to an oven and maintained at 120 °C for 5 h.

AuNPs/ITO electrode was cleaned by ethanol and water for three times, and finally dried under N2 stream. Then the Au/ITO electrode was immersed in a 20 mL solution of 6-mercaptohexanoic acid and 2-mercapto ethanol, (molar ratio 4/1) for 40 min. The electrode (MER/Au/ITO) was then rinsed with ethanol and water for several times. This electrode was immersed in a solution of a10 mL buffer of 0.01 MES (pH 6) containing 10 mL EDC/NHS (2;1) for 30 min. The immobilizing of the ssDNA on the activated MER/Au/ITO was carried out by dropping of 5 μL of 5 μM ssDNA solution on its surface and incubated for 2 h at ambient condition. Ultimately, the prepared (ssDNA/MER/Au/ITO) electrode stored in phosphate buffer at 4 °C. The aptasensor fabrication steps are shown in Scheme 1 .

In the hybridizing step, the ssDNA/MER/Au/ITO was transferred into the solution of different concentrations of tDNA and incubated for 45 min at room temperature. Then, the resulted dsDNA/MER/Au/ITO electrode was rinsed using PBS (pH 7.4) for the removal of the non-specifically adsorbed t-DNA.

In order to monitor the electrochemical behavior of ssDNA/MER/Au/ITO electrode toward tDNA, the differential pulse voltammetric (DPV) technique, based on “signal off” strategy, was utilized. Here, the ssDNA/MER/AuNPs/ITO electrode before and after the hybridization (dsDNA/MER/Au/ITO) with different concentrations of tDNA, the was transfer to 5 mL solution containing of 0.1 mg mL−1 CDs-Nr and the electrochemical signal of CDs-Nr (analytical probe) was recorded. The CDs-Nr was used as a cheap external probe with proper electrochemical behavior.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristics of the synthesized CDs and CDs modified with neutral red (CDs-Nr)
ce:section.ce:para: To monitor the synthesis of CDs and CDs-Nr, we used different techniques such as fluorescence UV–Vis, IR, DLS and as well as electrochemical technique (CV) were used.

Fig. 1 A shows the UV–Vis spectra of Nr, CDs and CDs-Nr. The Nr spectrum shows two peaks at 275 and 530 nm, which can be attributed to two forms of Nr (acidic and neutral) in water solution [18]. In fact, the absorption at lower wavelength can be attributed to benzene ring and the higher absorbance can be attributed to the conjugated 3-ring system in Nr [19]. The CDs has two absorption peaks in aqueous solution in the UV region, one in 244 nm (ascribed to the π-π* transition of CC) and the other at 307 nm (ascribed to the n-π* transition of CO) [20]. After reaction of CDs with Nr and synthesis of CDs-Nr, the related peak of Nr in 530 nm almost disappeared (it shifted to 545 nm) and the 244 nm peak (related to CDs) shifted to 224 nm. Moreover, the CDs-Nr shows another peak at 288 nm, which is located between the 307 nm (related to CDs) and 275 nm (related to Nr).

Since the fluorescence is an important characteristic of CDs, we used this technique to monitor the synthesis of CDs-Nr. As the Fig. 1B indicates, the fluorescence spectra of CDs (λex = 370) has a peak at 530 nm and Nr reveals a weak peak at 624 nm. However, the CDs-Nr, after reaction of CDs and Nr, shows a wide peak at 445 nm. The FTIR study also proves the successfully synthesis of CDs-Nr. As the Fig. 1C illustrates that the CDs have some peaks at 3414 cm−1 (OH stretching), 2919 cm−1 (symmetric CH2), 2847 cm−1 1621 cm−1 (CO, CC) (asymmetric CH2) and 1028 cm−1 (C–H). In addition, the Nr shows some characteristic peaks at 3414, 1621, 1505 and 1621 cm−1 which can be ascribed to the -N–H stretching in amide [21]. After synthesis of CDs-Nr via reaction between the activated carboxylic groups (from CDs) and amine groups (from Nr), the FT-IR spectrum of CDs-Nr shows significant decrease in peak at 3414 cm−1 (OH stretching) or 3414 cm−1 (NH stretching) suggesting successful formation of CDs-Nr via EDC/NHS reactions. Moreover, the DLS studies (Fig. 1D) shows the hydrodynamic CDs after modification with Nr and synthesis of CDs-Nr increases from 164 nm to 245 nm proving the successful preparation of our redox probe (CDs-Nr).

Electrochemical behavior of CDs, Nr and CDs-Nr were also investigated. As shown (Fig. S1A), the CVs of CDs, Nr and CDs-Nr were recorded in the 0.1 M PBS pH = 7.0) solution at potential window of −0.8 to −0.4 V, with a potential sweep rate of 100 mV s−1.

As it was expected, at this potential window, the CV of the CDs (0.1 mg mL−1) does not show any faradic current on the surface of an MER/Au/ITO electrode with surface area 0.0314 cm2. On the contrary, the Nr shows a quasireversible redox peaks at −0.65 and −0.59 V with ΔE of 0.06 V. As it has been reported, the Nr has a reversible electrochemical behavior accompanied with 2 electrons and 2H+ in its redox reaction [22]. Moreover, the CV of CDs-Nr (0.1 mg mL−1) also shows a stronger faradic peaks at −0.64 and −0.57 with ΔE of 0.07 V proving the suitable electrochemical activity of CDs-Nr as an electrochemical probe.

In addition to having a good electrochemical activity, an electrochemical probe must not adsorb at the surface of electrode, which its results is fouling effect. As shown in Fig. S1B the fouling effect is one of the problem of Nr. The MER/Au/ITO electrode was immersed in Nr solution and after removal it was washed with distilled water and its CV was recorded in PBS. As shown due to adsorption of Nr at the electrode surface a strong redox peaks in the CV test even after the further electrode washing can be observed. The same experiment was carried out with CDs-Nr solution. However, the MER/Au/ITO, shows no significant fouling effect towards CDs-Nr as an electrochemical probe that may be due to desorption of CDs-Nr during washing

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the modified ITO electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The different steps of surface modification of ITO electrode were monitored by AFM technique. As it is seen from Fig. 2 A the bare surface of electrode with its typical roughness [23] presenting an almost disordered topography in forward scan with a range of ±5 nm. As the Au layer deposited on the surface of electrode, a thin layer of Au is homogenously formed on the surface of ITO electrode and the morphology of ITO does not undergo significant changes (Fig. 2B). However, after the heating treatment, semispherical shapes of Au are observable on the surface of ITO and the forward scan shows distinguishable arrays with topography range of ±9 nm (Fig. 2C). After immobilization of DNA on the surface of the modified ITO (ssDNA/MER/Au/ITO) the AFM image shows that the surface morphology of electrode (Fig. 2D) is completely changed and the surface has almost homogenous layers of DNA probe.

In order to study the behavior of electrode during the modification steps and addition of probe and complementary DNA, we used EIS and CV techniques. As the Fig S2A illustrates, the EIS plots of bare ITO, Au/ITO, MER/Au/ITO, ssDNA/MER/Au/ITO, and dsDNA/MER/Au/ITO in the solution of 0.1 M phosphate buffer of pH 7.4 containing 2.0 mM of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4. The kinetics parameters (double layer capacitance, Cdl, solution resistance, Rs, apparent charge transfer rate constant, kapp, and charge transfer resistance, Rct) via applying an equvalent circuit (Fig. S2A inset) were calculated and the results are presented in Table 1 . Here, the bare ITO electrode shows a Rct of 381.01 Ω while its CV (Fig. S2B) has a two redox peaks at 0.23 and 0.08 V (ΔE = 0.15 V). After surface modification of ITO with Au, Rct of Au/ITO electrode decreases to 89.01 Ω and the kapp increased more than four times, which indicates the improvement of the electroactivity of Au/ITO electrode. Morover, the CV study shows the smaller ΔE (0.10 V) with higher redox peaks current. In addition, the Cdl also shows an increase which can be attributed to surface charge and area of gold nanostructures on the electrode [24]. When the surface of the Au/ITO modifed with thiolated groups, the CVstudy of MER/Au/ITO electrode shows smaller redox peak current with ΔE of 0.19 V. The EIS exprement also illustrates an increase in Rct parameter proving the attachment of thiol groups. Here, the Cdl also decreased that could be related to the changing in surface charge and area due to the attachment of MER groups. Finally, after the immobilization of DNA probe at the MER/Au/ITO electrode and its hybridization with target DNA, their Rct and peaks current were increased and decreased respectively that reveal the presence of ssDNA and dsDNA at the MER/Au/ITO electrode. The values of Cdl also increase, which proves the changes in the surface charge at ssDNA/MER/Au/ITO and dsDNA/MER/Au/ITO electrodes.

Since we used CDs-Nr as electrochemical probe for detection of the target DNA, we optimized the effect of DNA hybridization time via recording the DPV signal of CDs-Nr (0.1 mg mL−1) after different hybridization time and recording the electrochemical signal before and after hybridization (ΔI). The DNA hybridization time was investigated over the range of 15 – 75 min in the solution of 10 pM of the target. Fig. S3, shows that the maximum change in the analytical signal was obtained after the incubation time of 15 min. For longer times, the electrochemical signal of CDs-Nr did not significantly change, suggesting that the hybridization process was almost carried out. However, to be sure, we chose a time of 45 min as the optimized hybridization time.

The analytical calibration was performed using various concentrations of target DNA to evaluate the sensitivity of the fabricated biosensor. As can be seen from Fig. 3 A, DPV’s responses decreased linearly with the increasing target DNA concentration over the range 20–240 fM with a regression equation of Δi p (μA) = 0.0017C [DNA] + 0.1114 (R 2 = 0.996). The limit of detection (LOD) of this method was 10 fM (3 s).

The selectivity of the proposed DNA biosensor for target detection was studied by using double base mismatched (DMM) and non-complementary matched (NCMM) strands, using the DPV method and the results are presented in Table 2 . As seen, the corresponding ΔI values at an equimolar concentration of 300 fM were 25% (DMM), and 4% (NCMM).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simulated proof-of-concept demonstration using DNA fragments
ce:section.ce:para: We used a spiked method to study the biosensor applicability by simulating the real sample. By utilizing the different concentrations of tDNA, we obtained the standard deviation and recovery percentage after three time measuring (n = 3). The results are presented in Table 3 . As the results shows the sensor has a RSD about 4.9% to 3.6% with recovery of 105 to 102.5% which is a very acceptable results.

Table 4 compares the results of this work with other reported methods used for detecting Salmonella DNA. As the table suggests, the proposed methods has comparable or even better analytical performance in comparison with other works.

Excellent analytical performance of our proposed method may be attributed to the two aspects of this research. First, preparation of suitable modified surface with high surface area, which allowed us to, immobilized the recognition element on the surface of the electrode. Secondly, modified CDs acted as an effective electrochemical probe. The resulted biosensor proved its ability for low-level determination of the salmonella sequence in real sample.

Sajad Azizi: Formal analysis, original draft. Mohammad-Bagher Gholivand: Project administration, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, administration, Resources, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing, Data curation. Masoud Amiri: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Iraj Manouchehri: Writing - review & editing, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2020.105523.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
